metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
metadata.notes: Core/restored mushroom records retained at the original mushrooms.json path. Expanded mushroom records now load from smaller grouped files listed in split_manifest. v4.2.80 adds a core mushroom credibility pass: source links, use roles, foraging values, card notes, and cleaned placeholder use text for high-visibility mushrooms. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved. v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species: completed selected mushroom species records in-place with rights metadata and stale review flags cleared.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.
metadata.last_reaudit_note: Re-audited v4.3.61-v4.3.63 touched mushroom species against hard completion checklist; incomplete items explicitly declared instead of silently marked complete.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Wild velvet foot is edible when confidently identified and cooked, but it is a winter gilled mushroom with deadly look-alikes. Keep wild brown-stemmed forms separate from cultivated enoki in your head.
records.edibility_detail: Confirm clustered growth on wood, orange-brown sticky caps, white spore print, no ring, and a dark velvety stem base. Do not eat any Galerina-like brown gilled mushroom.
records.general_notes: The dark velvety stem base matters here. This is one of the few edible-type gilled mushrooms worth checking in cold weather.

mushroom_profile.season_note: October, November, December, January, February, March.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch C — gilled/ridged edibles and caution-adjacent species
records.field_identification: Small to medium orange-brown caps in clusters on dead hardwood, often in cold weather. Gills are pale, spore print white, there is no ring, and the lower stem becomes dark and velvety.
records.mushroom_card_note: Winter edible only with careful ID; deadly Galerina is the ugly little goblin in this room.

records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; do not confuse with jack-o'-lanterns or true-gilled orange mushrooms.
image_review_reasons: Photo rights metadata verified and completed in v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species.
records.review_note: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Species-complex wording should be kept conservative.
records.review_notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Species-complex wording should be kept conservative.
records.field_identification: Yellow chanterelle complex associated with conifer or mixed conifer woods. Look for blunt forked ridges/false gills running down the stem, firm solid flesh, a fruity apricot-like odor, and a white to pale yellow spore print.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conifer-associated yellow chanterelle complex; confirm blunt false gills, not true blade-like gills.

mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; conifer-associated yellow chanterelles should be verified to local species where possible.

records.edibility_detail: Edible chanterelle complex; use only when false gills, solid flesh, fruity odor, and habitat fit. Keep this as a species-complex record rather than pretending every yellow chanterelle is one species.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; check false gills, growth from soil, and lack of jack-o'-lantern clustering on wood.
image_review_reasons: Photo rights metadata verified and completed in v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species.

records.review_notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage; Species-complex wording should be kept conservative.
records.field_identification: Yellow chanterelle complex associated with hardwoods. Look for egg-yellow to orange-yellow caps, blunt forked ridges/false gills running down the stem, solid flesh, fruity apricot-like odor, and growth from soil rather than wood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hardwood yellow chanterelle complex; false gills/ridges are the key separator from jack-o'-lantern.

mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; hardwood-associated yellow chanterelles should be verified to local species where possible.

records.edibility_detail: Edible chanterelle complex; use only when the blunt false gills/ridges, solid flesh, fruity odor, and hardwood habitat fit. This stays intentionally conservative at complex level.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.edibility_detail: Do not eat. Contains psychoactive/toxic compounds and can cause serious illness, confusion, vomiting, and unsafe intoxication.
records.general_notes: A familiar northern Amanita and a useful safety record because it teaches cap warts, ring, white gills, and bulbous base checks.

edible_use.notes: Do not eat. Contains psychoactive/toxic compounds and can cause serious illness, confusion, vomiting, and unsafe intoxication.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch E — conks, chanterelle allies, and caution gilled record
records.scientific_name: Amanita muscaria var. guessowii / Amanita chrysoblema yellow-orange form
records.field_identification: Bright yellow to orange Amanita with white warts on the cap, white gills, ring on the stem, and a bulbous base with veil remnants. Often with birch, pine, spruce, or mixed woods in northern areas.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow/orange fly agaric: white warts, white gills, ring, bulbous base. Do not eat.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Amanita overview
use_links.notes: Amanita structures: volva, ring, white gills, universal veil remnants.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Good fresh or dried; small mushrooms dry well and rehydrate into soups and sauces.
records.general_notes: Useful late-season chanterelle relative. The hollow yellow foot and blunt ridges are better clues than cap color.
reviewReasons: Re-audit incomplete: photo coverage is below the 2-image minimum using exact-rights images.
records.field_identification: Small thin chanterelle relative with brownish cap, yellow to orange hollow stem, and blunt grayish-yellow ridges running down the underside. Often in mossy wet conifer or mixed woods.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small late-season chanterelle relative: hollow yellow foot and blunt decurrent ridges.

records.edibility_detail: Good edible after confirming blunt decurrent ridges, hollow yellowish stem, and soil/moss habitat. Avoid small brown gilled mushrooms with true gills.
